pattern: Since only the exact letters a word uses matter -- not their order, and never more than once each -- representing every word by the set of letters it contains turns 'append one new letter, then shuffle' into a simple one-letter-larger superset check.
hints: Order and repeated letters don't matter here -- think of every word as just the set of distinct letters it uses.
A target word is reachable exactly when removing one of its own letters leaves a set that matches some start word's letter set exactly.
Precompute the letter-set of every start word once into a lookup structure, then for each target try removing each of its own letters in turn and check the reduced set against that lookup.

constraints.note: each string is 1-26 lowercase letters with no letter repeated within it
each string is 1-26 lowercase letters with no letter repeated within it

edge_inputs.description: several distinct start words each unlocking exactly one target by adding a letter
smallest possible words, a single letter growing to two letters
a target that is the same letters as a start word (or a rearrangement of it) with nothing appended must not count
targets that are shorter, equal length, or two letters longer than the only start word are all unreachable -- only exactly one letter longer works
duplicate start words should not inflate the count -- reachability only needs one matching start word to exist
a target whose letters share nothing in common with any start word
a start word at the maximum word length growing by its one missing letter

stress.description: random distinct-letter words of varying length in both arrays
